title: Blink: prove the whole chain works
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Make the ESP32-S3's own onboard LED blink from code you upload — proof that your computer, the USB cable, the board, and the Arduino toolchain all talk to each other.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: A sketch is the Arduino name for a program. This lesson uses Freenove's Sketch_19.1_Ultrasonic_Ranging.ino so you can focus first on wiring, observation, and the measurement model.

coachInstructions: Keep the day to upload-and-watch; there is nothing to wire.
If nothing blinks, check board, port, and a data-capable cable before changing code.
Explain setup() vs loop() and the delay numbers on request; do not front-load theory.
For the challenge, have the learner change only the delay values and re-upload, preserving the working sketch.
steps: Plug the ESP32-S3 into your computer with a data-capable USB-C cable.
In Arduino IDE, select your ESP32-S3 board and the port it appears on.
Press Upload and wait for the IDE to say Done uploading.
Watch the small onboard LED near the chip start to blink.

arduino.explanation: Names the onboard LED's pin so the rest of the sketch reads plainly.
Runs once in setup() to say this pin will push signal out.
Turns the LED on; LOW turns it off.
Holds that state for one second before the next line.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Get ready once, then repeat on-wait-off-wait forever.
theoryModel.formula: one blink = 1 s on + 1 s off = a 2-second cycle

notes.title: setup() vs loop()
notes.body: setup() gets things ready a single time; loop() is the part that keeps running.

expectedOutputExample: The small onboard LED turns on for one second.
Then it repeats, steady, for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: The larger LED that stays lit is the power light — look for the smaller one that blinks.

firstChecks: Confirm the right board and port are selected, then upload again.
Try a different USB-C cable — some are charge-only and carry no data.
That's the power LED. The blink is a second, smaller LED near the chip.

challenge: Change the delay values and find a rhythm that feels like a calm heartbeat, then note the two numbers you chose.
logbookPrompts: What delay felt most like a heartbeat?
What was the fastest blink you could still see as separate flashes?
What would you want this LED to tell you in a real project?
